statement.markdown: Given an array of integers that is K sorted, i.e., every element is at most K positions away from its correct position in the sorted array, sort the array in ascending order. The array is 0-indexed and contains distinct elements. You can use any data structure to solve this problem, but the goal is to achieve the most efficient solution possible.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains two integers, N and K, where N is the number of elements in the array and K is the maximum distance of an element from its correct position. The second line contains N integers representing the array elements.
statement.outputFormat: The sorted array in ascending order, with each element on a new line.
statement.notes: The input array contains distinct elements.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array
The maximum distance of an element from its correct position

examples.explanation: The given array is [3, 1, 4, 2, 5]. After sorting, the array becomes [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].
The given array is [6, 5, 3, 1, 8, 7, 2]. After sorting, the array becomes [1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8].

hints.text: To solve this problem, we need to understand the properties of a K sorted array and how we can utilize them to sort the array efficiently. The key insight here is that every element is at most K positions away from its correct position in the sorted array.

Given the constraint that every element is at most K positions away from its correct position, we can consider using a data structure that allows us to efficiently find the minimum element within a certain range of the array, such as a heap or a priority queue. This could help us to iteratively extract the smallest element from the unsorted part of the array and place it in its correct position.

A suitable approach to solve this problem involves maintaining a min-heap of size K+1, where we initially push the first K+1 elements of the array into the heap. Then, we start extracting the minimum element from the heap and placing it in its correct position in the sorted array, while also pushing the next element from the array into the heap. This process continues until all elements have been sorted, resulting in a time complexity of O(N log K).

editorial.title: Efficient Sorting of K-Sorted Arrays
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved efficiently by leveraging the properties of k-sorted arrays and utilizing a min-heap data structure. A k-sorted array is one where each element is at most k positions away from its correct sorted position. This observation allows us to use a min-heap of size k to keep track of elements that are potentially out of place.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve sorting the entire array first, which would take O(n log n) time. This approach does not take advantage of the k-sorted property and is therefore inefficient for large arrays with small k.
editorial.betterSolution: By using a min-heap of size k, we can efficiently sort the array. We iterate through the array, pushing elements onto the heap. If the heap size exceeds k, we pop the smallest element from the heap and add it to the result. This ensures that the heap always contains the k smallest elements seen so far.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution utilizes a min-heap of size k to achieve a time complexity of O(n log k) and a space complexity of O(k). This approach takes full advantage of the k-sorted property of the array.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that the min-heap always contains the k smallest elements seen so far. When the heap size exceeds k, popping the smallest element from the heap ensures that the heap remains sorted and that the k smallest elements are always present. Once all elements have been processed, popping the remaining elements from the heap yields the sorted array.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the optimal solution with a sample input helps verify its correctness. For example, with the input [6, 5, 3, 2, 8, 10, 9] and k = 3, the solution correctly sorts the array.
commonMistakes: Not utilizing the k-sorted property of the array
Using a heap size larger than k, which would increase space complexity
Not handling the case where the heap size exceeds k
Assuming the input array is already sorted
Not verifying the correctness of the solution with sample inputs
